1: ʾallĕlî lî kî hāyîtî kĕʾaspĕsuppê-qayiṣ kĕʿōlĕlōt bāṣîr: “Woe is me! I am like a gleaner at the summer harvest, like one picking through the remnants of the vintage.” The opening ʾallĕlî is the lament-cry of the prophetic speaker; the harvest metaphors convey total exhaustion of moral resources — the summer harvest is stripped bare, and the gleaner finds only remnants (ʿōlĕlōt, the dropped clusters left for the poor by law, Leviticus 19:10; Deuteronomy 24:21). bikkûrāh ʾiwwĕtāh napšî — “my soul craved the first-ripe fruit (bikkûrāh)” — the first-ripe figs of early summer are the OT’s symbol of the best and most desired produce (Hosea 9:10: “like the first-ripe fig at the beginning of the season”); the prophet’s soul hungers for a faithful person and finds none. The harvest-exhaustion metaphor frames the social diagnosis of Micah 7:2-6.
2: ʾābad ḥāsîd min-hāʾāreṣ wĕyāšār bāʾādām ʾayin: “The faithful (ḥāsîd) have vanished from the land; there is no one upright (yāšār) among people.” Ḥāsîd is the covenant-loyal person, characterized by ḥesed (steadfast love) — the same root as YHWH’s own defining attribute (Micah 7:18: ḥāpeṣ ḥesed hûʾ). Its disappearance from the land is the covenant catastrophe: the community whose hallmark is ḥesed has lost its ḥăsîdîm. kullām lĕdāmîm yeʾĕrōbû (“they all lie in ambush for blood”) — dāmîm is the plural of blood, indicating bloodshed or blood-guilt (cf. 2 Samuel 3:28); yeʾĕrōbû from ʾārab = to lie in wait/ambush. The hunting-net image (yāṣûdû ʾîš ʾet-ʾāḥîw bĕḥērem, “each hunts his brother with a net”) inverts the covenant of brotherhood.
3: ʿal-hārāʿ kappayim lĕhêṭîb haśśar šōʾēl wĕhaššōpēṭ baššillûm: “They are diligent at doing evil — the prince demands a bribe, the judge accepts a payment.” ʿal-hārāʿ kappayim lĕhêṭîb is a compressed phrase: “for evil, [using] their hands, to do well/excel at it” — they have perfected the art of evil. The three corruptors (prince, judge, powerful man) operate as a syndicate: the powerful man (haggādôl) articulates the demand (habbîa ʿnapšô hîʾ, “states the craving of his soul”) and together they “weave/twist” it (wayyĕʿabbĕṭûhā — from ʿābat, to twist or distort; the verb is rare and the text difficult; the image is of a scheme woven from multiple threads of corruption).
4: ṭôbām kamĕmēr yāšār min-mĕsûkāh: “The best among them is like a briar; the most upright is sharper than a thorn hedge.” The superlative corruption: even the best (ṭôbām) in this society is a briar (mĕmēr — a thorny briar-plant, elsewhere ḥēdaeq). The thorn-hedge (mĕsûkāh) is the standard barrier against intruders — thorny plants used for fencing. The social fabric, which should be like clothing that protects, has become only thorns that injure. yôm mĕṣappêkā pĕquddātĕkā bāʾāh — “the day your watchmen warned of — your time of judgment — has arrived”; the “watchmen” (mĕṣappêkā) are the prophets who warned of exactly this (cf. Isaiah 52:8; Ezekiel 3:17) — pĕquddāh = appointed time/visitation/reckoning. ʿattāh tihyeh mĕbûkātām (“now they are thrown into bewilderment”) — the rulers who were confident face sudden confusion.
5: ʾal-taʾamînû bĕrēaʿ ʾal-tiṭĕḥû bĕʾallûp: “Put no trust in a neighbor; place no confidence in a close friend.” Rēaʿ = neighbor/companion (the covenant relationship of Leviticus 19:18: “love your neighbor”); ʾallûp = a close companion, intimate friend (used in Psalms 55:13 of a trusted companion who betrays). The breaking of social trust at every level — neighbor, companion, even spouse (“guard what you say even from the one who lies in your arms”, šōkevet ḥêqekā) — mirrors the complete social breakdown of Micah 7:2-4. This extreme counsel (not trusting anyone) is prophetic description of a social condition, not a timeless maxim against trust.
6: kî-ben mĕnabbēl ʾāb bat qāmāh vĕʾimmāh kallāh bĕḥōtantāh: “For the son treats his father with contempt, the daughter rises against her mother, the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.” The household-division catalogue reaches its climax: ʾōyĕbê ʾîš ʾanšê bêtô — “a person’s enemies are the members of their own household.” Jesus cites this verse directly in Matthew 10:35-36: “I have come to bring not peace but a sword. For I have come to bring division — a son against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law. A man’s enemies will be those of his own household.” Jesus takes Micah’s description of social collapse and applies it to the division that his proclamation will produce — not as the goal of his mission but as its inevitable effect in a world resistant to the kingdom. Luke 12:51-53 parallels with “Do you think I came to give peace on earth? No, I tell you, but rather division.”
7: wĕʾānî bYHWH ʾăṣappeh ʾôḥîlāh lēʾlōhê yišʿî: “As for me, I will watch expectantly for the LORD; I will wait for the God who saves me.” After the social chaos of Micah 7:1-6, the individual speaker pivots on the strong adversative wĕʾānî (“but as for me” — the same pronoun-pivot as Psalms 73:23; Psalms 5:7). ʾăṣappeh from ṣāpāh = to watch/look out (the watchman's posture); ʾôḥîlāh from yāḥal = to wait with hopeful expectation. ʾĕlōhê yišʿî (“the God of my salvation”) — yišʿî = my salvation (root yšʿ, also in Jonah 2:9 and the name Jesus/Yeshua). The pivot from communal despair (Micah 7:1-6) to personal trust (Micah 7:7) is the structure of prophetic lament — the “but as for me” that refuses to be defined by the surrounding conditions.
8: ʾal-tišmĕḥî ʾōyabtî lî kî nāpaltî qamtî kî-ʾēšēb baḥōšek YHWH ʾôr lî: “Do not gloat over me, my enemy. Though I have fallen, I will rise. Though I sit in darkness, the LORD is a light for me.” The individual voice (Micah 7:7) now addresses an enemy — whether Assyria, Babylon, or any hostile nation is deliberately left general. Two profound antitheses: (1) kî nāpaltî qamtî — “though I have fallen, I will rise” — the qāmtî (I will arise/rise) is the verb of resurrection and awakening (Daniel 12:2: yāqîṣû; Isaiah 26:19: “your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise”); (2) ʾēšēb baḥōšek YHWH ʾôr lî — darkness-to-light by YHWH’s presence. Isaiah 9:2 (cited in Matthew 4:16) uses the same darkness-to-light structure for the Messianic arrival. The ʾôr lî (“light to me”) is YHWH himself as light — cf. Psalms 27:1: “YHWH is my light and my salvation.”
9: zaʿap YHWH ʾeśśāʾ kî ḥāṭāʾtî lô ʿad ʾăšer yārîb rîbî wĕʿāśāh mišpāṭî: “I will endure the LORD’s anger, for I have sinned against him, until he takes up my case and brings justice for me.” The legal language is precise: yārîb rîbî (“he will argue/contend my case”) — YHWH as the gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) and legal advocate who takes up the case of the one wrongly accused. rîb is the covenant-lawsuit term used throughout the prophets (Micah 6:2; Jeremiah 2:9; Hosea 4:1). wĕʿāśāh mišpāṭî (“brings justice for me”) — the mišpāṭ YHWH establishes is both vindication of the wronged party and condemnation of the oppressor. yōṣîʾēnî lāʾôr (“he will lead me into the light”) resumes the darkness-light of Micah 7:8: the light is YHWH’s just verdict made visible.
10: wĕtirʾeh ʾōyabtî ûtĕkassehā bûšāh: “My enemy will see it; shame will cover the one who taunted me.” The reversal: the one who mocked becomes the one shamed. The taunt (haggîd ʾelay ʾayyē YHWH ʾĕlōhāyik, “who said to me, ‘Where is the LORD your God?’”) is the standard theodicy-taunt of enemies (Psalms 42:3, 10: “Where is your God?”; Psalms 79:10: “Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’”; Joel 2:17). The taunt assumes YHWH’s absence from the humiliated people; the reversal (“my own eyes will look upon her; now she will be trampled like mud”, tîrāmās kĕṭît ḥûṣôt) is the vindication that silences the taunt. The mud-trampling image echoes Micah 7:17 (nations lick dust like snakes).
11: yôm libnôt gĕdērāyik yôm hahûʾ yirḥaq ḥōq: “A day is coming for rebuilding your walls; on that day the foreign decree will be lifted far away.” The restoration oracle: libnôt gĕdērāyik — “to build your walls” (the walls of the city, whether Jerusalem’s literal walls or the walls as metaphor for protection and sovereignty). yirḥaq ḥōq (“the decree will be lifted far”) — the ḥōq here is the foreign decree of empire (perhaps the Assyrian or Babylonian administrative/tribute law imposed on vassal states); its removal signals genuine restoration of sovereignty. The “day” (yôm hahûʾ) is the eschatological “that day” of prophetic hope.
12: yôm hahûʾ wĕyābōʾ ʾādêkā lĕminnî ʾaššûr wĕʿārê miṣrayim: “In that day people will come to you from Assyria and the cities of Egypt — from Egypt to the Euphrates, from sea to sea and from mountain to mountain.” The universal scope of restoration: the streaming of peoples from Assyria and Egypt (the two great empires that dominated Israel’s history) to Zion parallels Isaiah 2:2-3 and Micah 4:1-2 (the Micah passage almost identical to Isa 2). The miyyām ʿad-yām ûmihhar ʿad-hāhār (“from sea to sea and mountain to mountain”) is the idiom for universal scope — the same as Psalms 72:8 (“may he have dominion from sea to sea”), applied to the messianic king. The international pilgrimage to Zion that Mic 4 announced is here restated in the context of personal and national restoration.
13: wĕhāyĕtāh hāʾāreṣ lĕšammāh ʿal-yōšĕbeyhā mippĕrî maʿallêhem: “But the land will become desolate because of its inhabitants — the result of their deeds.” A parenthetical note of judgment within the restoration oracle: the land (hāʾāreṣ) — the land from which the peoples stream in Micah 7:12, or perhaps the whole earth — will experience desolation (šammāh) proportional to its inhabitants’ deeds (mippĕrî maʿallêhem, “from the fruit of their deeds” — the harvest-consequence image resuming Micah 7:1). The agricultural metaphor (fruit → desolation) closes the harvest imagery of Micah 7:1-4.
14: rĕʿēh ʿammĕkā bĕšibṭekā ṣōʾn naḥălātĕkā: “Shepherd your people with your staff — the flock of your inheritance — who dwell isolated in a forest surrounded by fertile land.” The prophet addresses YHWH directly: rĕʿēh (“shepherd!” — the imperative of pastoral care). Šibeṭ = staff/rod/scepter — simultaneously the shepherd’s crook and the ruler’s scepter (Psalms 2:9; Micah 23:4); the two meanings coexist. ṣōʾn naḥălātĕkā (“flock of your inheritance”) — YHWH’s people as his naḥălāh (inheritance/possession), echoing Deuteronomy 9:26 (“do not destroy your people, your inheritance”). šōknî lĕbādād yaar bĕtôk karmel — “who dwell isolated in a forest surrounded by fertile land” — the isolation of the remnant in difficult terrain, waiting for YHWH’s restoration. Bashan and Gilead = the ideal northern pastures (cf. Jeremiah 50:19; the Transjordanian highlands famous for lush grazing).
15: kimê ṣēʾtkā mēʾereṣ miṣrayim ʾarʾennû niplāʾôt: “As in the days when you came out of the land of Egypt, I will show them wonders (niplāʾôt).” YHWH responds (the speaker shifts from prophet to YHWH, or is understood as YHWH in the prophet’s vision): the new redemption will be structured like the Exodus. Niplāʾôt (wonders/miracles — from the root pālaʾ, “to be extraordinary/wonderful”) is the technical term for the Exodus plagues and sea-crossing (Exodus 3:20; Psalms 78:12; Micah 106:7). The new Exodus promise — structured identically to the first — is a central NT category: Jesus’s transfiguration (“departure”/exodos, Luke 9:31), his feeding miracles (Exodus-manna typology), and his death and resurrection as the ultimate Exodus fulfillment.
16: yirʾû gôyim wĕyēbōšû mikkōl gĕbûrātām: “The nations will see and be put to shame despite all their power.” The vision of universal shame (bōšâ) in the face of YHWH’s powerful restoration: the nations who taunt (“where is your God?”, Micah 7:10) see the wonders (Micah 7:15) and are silenced. yāśîmû yād ʿal-peh (“they put their hand over their mouth”) — the gesture of speechlessness before the overwhelming; used in Job 29:9 (the great honor the elders showed Job); Micah 40:4 (Job’s response to YHWH from the whirlwind). ʾāzněhem tĕḥĕraš (“their ears will be stopped/deafened”) — the silence extends to their hearing as well as their speaking.
17: yĕlaḥăkû ʿāpār kannāḥāš kĕziḥălê ʾereṣ yirgĕzû mimmisĕgĕrôtêhem: “They will lick the dust like a snake, like creatures that crawl on the earth; they will come trembling out of their fortresses.” The snake-and-dust image deliberately reverses Genesis 3:14 (the curse on the serpent: “you will eat dust all the days of your life”) — the nations take the serpent’s posture of defeat. Yĕlaḥăkû ʿāpār (“lick the dust”) is the posture of total submission; Psalms 72:9 uses it of the king’s enemies: “may his enemies lick the dust.” yipḥădû ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû wĕyirʾû mimmekā (“they will turn in dread to the LORD our God and will be in fear of you”) — the nations’ submission is not yet salvation; it is the fear of overwhelming power that precedes (or stands apart from) covenant inclusion.
18: mî-ʾēl kāmôkā nōśēʾ ʿāwôn wĕʿōbēr ʿal-pešaʿ lišʾērît naḥălātô: “Who is a God like you, forgiving iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of his inheritance?” The name Mîkāh means “who is like YHWH?” (mî kāh/mî kamōhû) — the book’s own name is embedded in this climactic question. Mî-ʾēl kāmôkā is the rhetorical answer to YHWH’s uniqueness: no one. The divine action is described with two verb phrases: (1) nōśēʾ ʿāwôn (“bearing/lifting iniquity”) — the same root (nāśāʾ) as Isaiah 53:12: “he bore the sins of many” (wĕhûʾ ḥēṭʾ-rabbîm nāśāʾ); the bearing-iniquity language of the Suffering Servant pre-echoed here; (2) ʿōbēr ʿal-pešaʿ (“passing over transgression”) — ʿābar in this context = overlooking, not requiring payment for (cf. Proverbs 19:11). kî-ḥāpeṣ ḥesed hûʾ (“for he delights in steadfast love”) — YHWH’s ḥesed is his ḥēpeṣ (desire/delight), not a reluctant concession.
19: yāšûb yĕraḥămēnû yikbōš ʿăwōnōtênû wĕtašlîk bimĕṣûlôt yām kol-ḥaṭṭōʾtām: “He will again have compassion on us; he will trample our iniquities underfoot. You will hurl all our sins into the depths of the sea.” Three verbs of divine action over sin: (1) yāšûb yĕraḥămēnû — “he will return to have compassion” (šûb + raḥam); (2) yikbōš ʿăwōnōtênû — “he will subdue/conquer our iniquities” (kābaš = to tread down, subdue; used of the creation mandate in Genesis 1:28 and of military conquest); the iniquities are treated as an enemy to be conquered; (3) wĕtašlîk bimĕṣûlôt yām kol-ḥaṭṭōʾtām — “you will hurl (tašlîk) all their sins into the depths of the sea.” The verb šālak (hurl/throw) is the same root as Jonah 1:15 (throwing Jonah into the sea) and Jeremiah 36:23 (throwing Jeremiah’s scroll into the fire). The depths of the sea (mĕṣûlôt yām) = the place of no return; the Exodus sea that swallowed Pharaoh’s army (Exodus 15:5) becomes the place where YHWH hurls sin itself. This verse became the basis for the Jewish practice of Tashlikh (casting bread on water on Rosh Hashanah) and is the OT’s most vivid image of the totality of forgiveness.
20: tittēn ʾĕmet lĕYaʿăqōb ḥesed lĕʾAbrāhām ʾăšer-nišbaʿtā laʾăbōtênû mîmê qedem: “You will be faithful (ʾĕmet) to Jacob and show steadfast love (ḥesed) to Abraham, just as you swore to our fathers in days long past.” The final verse anchors the entire eschatological hope in the patriarchal covenants. ʾĕmet (faithfulness/truth) for Jacob and ḥesed (steadfast covenant love) for Abraham — the two covenantal attributes of YHWH (Exodus 34:6: “abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness”, rav ḥesed wĕʾĕmet) applied to the two patriarchs. Nišbaʿtā (you swore by oath) — the Abrahamic covenant confirmed by divine oath (Genesis 22:16-17: “by myself I have sworn”; Hebrews 6:13-18: the oath guarantees the promise unchangeable). Paul builds his entire theology of the gospel upon exactly this: the promise to Abraham (ḥesed lĕʾAbrāhām) is fulfilled in Christ, who is Abraham’s seed (Galatians 3:16), and through whom all nations receive the blessing sworn to the fathers (Galatians 3:14). The book of Micah ends where Luke 1:72-73 begins: “to show the mercy (ἔλεος = LXX’s rendering of ḥesed) promised to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant, the oath that he swore to our father Abraham.”
